coredata.dc:title: Self-assembly and phase transition of gold nanoclusters in natural deep eutectic solvent for visual detection of toxicants in water environment
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Jianqiang
Zhao, Ning
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: Ecological damage derived from water pollution has increased greatly accompanying by the development of industries, which promotes the exploration of water environment monitoring platform. Structure-oriented synthesis of gold nanoclusters (NADES-Au NCs) based on novel natural deep eutectic solvent (NADES), has been successfully obtained without the use of any stabilizer or capping agents. With the pH stability and photobleaching resistance in the complex environment, spongy NADES-Au NCs showed superior optical properties than other forms. Moreover, this green sensing platform based on NADES-Au NCs displayed two well-separated emission peaks at 440 and 669 nm, respectively. There was a linear range of 0.5–200 µM with a detection limit of 0.25 µM for Hg2+ and a linear range of 1–150 µM with a detection limit of 0.12 µM for nitroanilines. Such NADES-Au NCs demonstrated the satisfactory recoveries and reliabilities in water samples, which presented great potential of water pollution detection. Simultaneously, the application of NADES as a green solvent for the synthesis of metal nanoclusters may provide new avenues for sensing platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Natural Deep Eutectic Solvent
Stabilizer
Gold nanoclusters
Hg2+
Nitroanilines

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NADES stabilized NADES-Au NCs
The quenching mechanism of NADES-Au NCs
The analytical performance of the method
Selectivity and anti-interference of the method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of Au NCs. (B) The TEM image and the particle size distribution histogram of the NADES-Au NCs. The SEM images of (C) Au NCs and (D) NADES-Au NCs.
(A) The XPS full scan spectrum of the NADES-Au NCs. High-resolution spectra of C 1s (B), N 1s (C), O 1s (D) and Au 4f (E). (F) The corresponding FI-IR spectra of NADES-Au NCs, NADES and Au NCs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of NADES-Au NCs (Abs). (B) The fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of NADES-Au NCs. (Inset: The corresponding photographs under sunlight (left), UV irradiation of 375 nm). Comparison of fluorescence intensity of NADES-Au NCs and Au NCs under different pH (C) and continuous UV lamp irradiation (D).
Influence of solution pH value (A), reaction time (B) and temperature (C) on the fluorescence intensity of NADES-Au NCs after the addition of Hg2+ and 4-NA.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of NADES-Au NCs before and after adding 200 µM Hg2+ and 150 µM 4-NA. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NA and the excitation and emission spectra of NADES-Au NCs. (C) The fluorescence decay curves of NADES-Au NCs in the presence and in the absence of Hg2+ and 4-NA. (D) The zeta potentials of NADES-Au NCs with and without Hg2+ and 4-NA.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of NADES-Au NCs in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ and (B) linear relationship. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra and (D) linear diagram of NADES-Au NCs with increasing concentration of 4-NA. (E) and (F) Fluorescence map and visualization of NADES-Au NCs in the presence of Hg2+ and 4-NA.

(A) The F1/F2 values of NADES-Au NCs in the presence of Hg2+ and various possible interfering substances (1 mM). (B) Fluorescence intensities of NADES-Au NCs towards 4-NA with other interferents (500 µM).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis process and characteristics of NADES-Au NCs and the sensing strategies for the detection of Hg2+ and nitroanilines.

head.ce:title: Self-assembly and phase transition of gold nanoclusters in natural deep eutectic solvent for visual detection of toxicants in water environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016 Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process and characteristics of NADES-Au NCs and the sensing strategies for the detection of Hg2+ and nitroanilines.

Ecological damage derived from water pollution has increased greatly accompanying by the development of industries, which promotes the exploration of water environment monitoring platform. Structure-oriented synthesis of gold nanoclusters (NADES-Au NCs) based on novel natural deep eutectic solvent (NADES), has been successfully obtained without the use of any stabilizer or capping agents. With the pH stability and photobleaching resistance in the complex environment, spongy NADES-Au NCs showed superior optical properties than other forms. Moreover, this green sensing platform based on NADES-Au NCs displayed two well-separated emission peaks at 440 and 669 nm, respectively. There was a linear range of 0.5–200 µM with a detection limit of 0.25 µM for Hg2+ and a linear range of 1–150 µM with a detection limit of 0.12 µM for nitroanilines. Such NADES-Au NCs demonstrated the satisfactory recoveries and reliabilities in water samples, which presented great potential of water pollution detection. Simultaneously, the application of NADES as a green solvent for the synthesis of metal nanoclusters may provide new avenues for sensing platform.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of economy and industry, heavy metal pollution discharged from industrial production has become an urgent issue, which causes severe impact on the environment and threatens human health. Mercury (II) (Hg2+), as one of the global toxic metal pollutants, is the main form of mercury pollution in water system with volatile and water-soluble properties [1]. Hg2+ is difficult to be biodegraded [2] and can be accumulated in the food chain. More seriously, Hg2+ may easily accumulate in liver along the biological chain, which can further cause the disorder of brain, kidney and the damage of central nervous system, endocrine system and other organs [3,4]. Nitroanilines are typical raw materials that have won wide applications on the synthesis of dyes, cosmetics, rubber, gas as well as others in industrial fields [5]. Within this class, 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) is an important component of toxic and harmful wastewater in an industrial environment. Due to the existence of powerful electron-absorbing groups nitro (–NO2), 4-NA and its analogs exhibit poor biodegradability, which can easily penetrate and accumulate in soil and groundwater. They can cause long-term damage to the environment [6,7], and then increase the risks of mutagenic, carcinogenic, and teratogenic effects on human body [8]. At present, environmental protection agencies, such as US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), regard it as a priority pollutant [9]. Consequently, it is an urgent demand to monitor the content of Hg2+ and nitroanilines in environment water.

In the past few years, various analytical techniques have been established to detect Hg2+, such as atomic absorption and emission spectroscopy (AAS, AES) [10], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [11] and so on. Moreover, currently reported detection methods for nitroanilines include molecular absorption spectrometry [12], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13], Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy (SERS) [14] and electrochemical method [15]. However, these methods have the limitations of complex sample pretreatment and expensive instrumentation, which greatly limit their application in environmental monitoring. In recent years, researchers have developed many new detection techniques, such as fluorescence analysis [16]. Fluorescence method has the advantages of simple operation, high cost effectiveness, real-time analysis [17], and can be used as the preferred choice for the detection of Hg2+ and nitroanilines.

Compared with conventional fluorescence sensors, novel metal nanoclusters (NCs), especially Au NCs consisting of a few to several hundred Au atoms, exhibit molecular properties of discrete electronic states [18]. Due to ultrasmall diameter, size-dependent photoluminescence and unique optical properties, Au NCs possess promising applications in the fields of sensing [19,20]. As we all know, the activity of Au NCs is mainly dependent on the microenvironment provided by proteins [21]. Meanwhile, Au NCs also have low quantum yield and poor stability that restrict their practical applications [22]. Now, some synthesis methods for controlling size and shape of Au NCs have been extensively developed in view of the particle surface structure and functional properties [23]. At the moment, natural deep eutectic solvent (NADES) emerges as a new type of green solvent that obtained by self-association through hydrogen bond interaction in the proper ratio [24]. NADES is regarded as promising and hydrogen-bond system “supramolecular” solvent, which can be used in shape-controlled synthesis of well-defined nanoparticles [25]. Furthermore, NADES is also used as a solvent and stabilizer to provide a stable environment and excellent luminescence properties for the synthesis of novel NADES-Au NCs.

Herein, NADES as solvent and stabilizer was introduced into the synthesis environment of Au NCs, and then a new green stable fluorescence material (NADES-Au NCs) was successfully obtained. The synthesized NADES-Au NCs inherited the excellent properties of Au NCs and NADES. It exhibited superior optical property and fluorescence stability in complex system and was used for detecting pollutants in the environment (Scheme 1 ). In the presence of Hg2+ or nitroanilines, NADES-Au NCs had different quenching types, including fluorescence quenching induced by Hg2+ at 669 nm and fluorescence quenching by nitroanilines at 440 nm and 669 nm. At the same time, fluorescence color changes from red to blue and red to light appeared respectively. Moreover, the method showed the wide linear ranges and low detection limits, which could be used for the determination of Hg2+ and nitroanilines in environmental water samples.

4-nitroaniline (4-NA), 2, 4-dinitroaniline (2, 4-NA), 2, 6-dinitroaniline (2, 6-NA) and other interference analog reagents were purchased from Yien Chemical Technology Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·3H2O), Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Choline chloride (ChCl) and glycerol (Gly) were bought from Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). Hg(NO3)2, AgNO3, Al(NO3)3, Ba(NO3)2, CdCl2, Co(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, KCl, LiNO3, Mg(NO3)2, Mn(NO3)2, NaCl, Ni(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2 were provided by Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co., ltd (Tianjin, China). NaOH and HCl were made by Tianjin Pharmaceutical Chemical Co., ltd (Tianjin, China). All reagents used were analytical grade, and Wahaha purified water was used throughout the experiment.

The oxidation states of Au clusters were collected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on Escalab250Xi (Thermo Fisher, USA). The morphology of the NADES-Au NCs was performed by using Tecnai G2 F30 (USA) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The morphology of the materials was inspected by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss GeminiSEM 300, Germany). And the UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on UV-5500PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Metash Instruments, China). All the fluorescence spectra of the NADES-Au NCs were recorded on a F97 using excitation wavelength at 375 nm (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., ltd. China). A Nicolet iN10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) was carried out to measure FT-IR and study functional group information of NADES-Au NCs. The absolute quantum yield (QY) and the lifetime of NADES-Au NCs were measured with an integrating sphere (Hamamatsu Photonics, Japan).

According to the usual procedure, the NADES was prepared by magnetic stirring with ChCl and Gly (with a molar ratio of 1:3) at 80 °C until a uniform and colorless liquid phase was obtained. Upon cooling down to room temperature naturally, it was placed in a closed vial and subsequently used for further synthesis work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NADES stabilized NADES-Au NCs
ce:section.ce:para: NADES-Au NCs was synthesized based on method described in literature with slight modification [26]. The difference was that NADES was used as the solvent and stabilizer. All glassware was rinsed and washed with ethanol and ultrapure water. In brief, NADES solution was prepared by mixing NADES with water at a volume ratio of 3:7. Then, 250 mg of BSA and 16.99 mg of HAuCl4 were ultrasonically dissolved in the NADES solution, to prepare for the next reaction in a round-bottomed flask. After two minutes of reaction, NaOH solution (0.5 mL, 1 M) was added rapidly to the mixture solution. Subsequently, the mixture was vigorously stirred at 50 °C for 6 h, until the solution changed from yellow to brown. Afterward, the final product was purified by centrifuging at 4000 rpm for 10 min, followed by a 0.22 µm filter membrane to separate the large particles. The NADES-Au NCs was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

100 µL of the as-synthesized NADES-Au NCs was mixed with 400 µL freshly prepared Hg2+ at different concentrations (0.5–200 µM). Then the above mixed solutions were diluted to 1 mL with phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH = 9) in centrifuge tube, respectively. Similar to the determination of Hg2+, the 4-NA was added to required concentration of NADES-Au NCs solution and shaken thoroughly at same condition prior to fluorescence measurement. Subsequently, the color changes after adding Hg2+ and 4-NA were observed by camera under UV lamp. And the fluorescence spectra from 400 to 800 nm were collected at 375 nm excitation wavelength.

The selectivity and interference of the sensing system with NADES-Au NCs probe were measured in the presence of target analyte or other interfering substances. Among them, the fluorescence ratios (F1/F2) were used to determine Hg2+, where F1 and F2 represented fluorescence signal at 440 nm and 669 nm, respectively. And the fluorescence intensity at 669 nm was used for the determination of 4-NA. All data were obtained by three independent measurements using similar procedures.

To evaluate the practical feasibility of as-prepared NADES-Au NCs, Hg2+ and nitroanilines in water were detected. The tap water and lake water were obtained respectively from our laboratory and campus, and then were filtered by a 0.22 µm filter membrane to rule out the interference of particulate matter. In the recovery experiment, the desired concentrations of Hg2+ (10, 100, 200 µM) and 4-NA (10, 100, 150 µM) were spiked with water samples and the resultant fluorescence spectra were recorded.

The morphology and particle size of the NADES-Au NCs were firstly characterized by TEM. Fig. 1 A showed the Au NCs synthesized without using NADES. And Fig. 1B showed a new type of NADES-AuNCs synthesized with NADES in this experiment. NADES-AuNCs was well-dispersed in aqueous solution compared with Au NCs, showing super-small spherical metal NCs with the particle size range of 1.0–2.4 nm. The introduction of NADES effectively inhibited the aggregation of Au NCs, resulting in better dispersion and morphology. Moreover, to reveal the changes of their surface morphology, the SEM patterns of the NADES-Au NCs and Au NCs were studied. According to the reports, NADES is a kind of promising solvents to be used in shape-controlled synthesis of nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 1C, Au NCs synthesized with water exhibited a distinct blocky distribution, which was similar to that reported in the literature [27]. However, unlike conventional solvents, the morphology of NADES-Au NCs has been greatly changed, forming a stagger-spongy distribution (Fig. 1D). It could be speculated that the sponge mesh formed by the introduction of NADES could protect the NADES-Au NCs from the external environment, and a large number of hydrogen bonds in their structures could interact with the NADES-Au NCs, which further enhanced the stability of the nanomaterials [28].

The FT-IR spectra of the surface functional groups of NADES, Au NCs and NADES-Au NCs were presented in Fig. 2F. The characteristic peaks of NADES-Au NCs around 3430 cm−1 became smooth and wide, verifying the formation of strong hydrogen bonds characteristic peak similar to NADES [34]. In addition, the typical unsaturated hydrocarbon stretching vibration peak at 2854 cm−1 also decreased, which were further confirmed the formation of strong hydrogen bonds. Meanwhile, the peaks observed at 1159 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric stretching vibration of CN and the stretching vibration of CO, respectively [35]. Furthermore, another absorption peak located at 670 cm−1 was defined the C-Cl group [36]. These results confirmed that NADES-Au NCs embedded a large number of oxygen functional groups and formed strong hydrogen bonds, thus ensuring its stability in the environment. These results from FT-IR spectroscopy were consistent with XPS data.

To explore optical properties of NADES-Au NCs, the UV–vis absorption of the NADES-Au NCs was shown in Fig. 3A. As demonstrated in UV–vis spectra (green part), the absorption peak centered at 270 nm was assigned to π-π* [37]. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescent spectrum exhibited three emission peaks, including a blue emission peak at 440 nm, a red emission peak at 669 nm and an accompanying independent frequency-doubling peak at 745 nm. Meanwhile, under the optimal excitation wavelength (375 nm), the emission peaks at 440 and 669 nm showed different properties, including excitation wavelength dependence at 440 nm (in the 400–450 nm range) and excitation wavelength independence at 669 nm.

Afterwards, the QY of Au NCs was measured to be 12.2 %. However, the QY of NADES-Au NCs was as high as 15.6 %, which may be due to the introduction of N, Cl elements and the reduction of the fluidity in NADES. This data was particularly prominent among the reported red emissive nanocluster. As displayed in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity of Au NCs (black line) showed an obvious downward trend under continuous light excitation by UV light for 90 min. Whereas the NADES-Au NCs (red line) had great photobleaching resistance, indicating that the improved material possessed good photostability. Moreover, we examined the effect of pH values on the fluorescence intensity of Au NCs and NADES-Au NCs. The as-prepared NADES-Au NCs (red line) presented strong fluorescence stability in the range of pH 6 to 11, which was beneficial for preservation and fluorescent sensing (Fig. 3D) and also overcame the instability of Au NCs (black line) under strong base to some extent. These results indicated that NADES-Au NCs had great potential for sensing applications under complex environments.

The synthetic NADES-Au NCs were used for follow-up research. In order to obtain the superior analytical performance for the determination of Hg2+ and nitroanilines, the experimental conditions were optimized, including the pH, time and temperature. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F1/F2) was evaluated for determination of Hg2+. Since the fluorescence intensities at 440 nm and 669 nm were decreased simultaneously by 4-NA (4-NA as a representative of nitroanilines), then the fluorescence intensity change at 669 nm was used to determine 4-NA. As shown in Fig. 4A, after the introduction of Hg2+ and 4-NA, the fluorescence intensities of NADES-Au NCs remained basically stable in the pH range of 5 to 8. So, we subsequently selected pH = 7 as the analytical detection condition. Furthermore, Fig. 4B showed the optimization process of the reaction time. After adding two objects, a sharp decrease of the fluorescence intensity could be basically observed within the first 1 min, and then the fluorescence intensity remained stable after 2 min. Hence 1 min was both selected as the optimal reaction time. Due to the rapid reaction time, the system was not affected by temperature changes (Fig. 4C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching mechanism of NADES-Au NCs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the importance of ligand-cluster interaction, the interaction of supporting solvent should also be considered in the synthesis of nanometer materials [24]. For example, published data [38] showed that gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) synthesized with NADES as a stabilizer could strongly interact with NADES solvent, resulting in the successful synthesis of star Au NPs bounded by (331) and ortho high refractive index crystal planes. In this paper, NADES-Au NCs was successfully synthesized by introducing NADES as solvent and stabilizer. The UV–vis absorption peak at 520 nm was not observed, indicating that there was no surface plasma peak. This result ruled out the synthesis of Au NPs. It was speculated that NADES increased the viscosity of the solution system, cut down the chance of intermolecular collisions, and then reduced the nonradiative transitions, thereby improving the fluorescence properties of NADES-Au NCs [39,40]. In addition, its strong hydrogen bonding system also ensured the stability of NADES-Au NCs in complex environments.

The feasibility of determination of Hg2+ and 4-NA was further discussed. Fig. 5 A showed the change of fluorescence intensity of NADES-Au NCs with the increase of Hg2+ and 4-NA concentrations. In order to investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of NADES-Au NCs, the UV–vis absorption spectra of NADES-Au NCs with the addition of the analytes were first studied. As displayed in Fig. 5B, the absorption spectrum of 4-NA overlapped perfectly with the excitation of NADES-Au NCs, which could be preliminarily attributed to the inner filtration effect (IFE). To confirm this speculation, the fluorescence lifetime of NADES-Au NCs before and after the addition of the 4-NA was studied. It could be seen from Fig. 5C that the average fluorescence lifetime of NADES-Au NCs in the presence of 4-NA was basically unchanged, further confirming the existence of IFE.

To further verify the quenching mechanism of Hg2+, several studies were performed. The fluorescence quenching of NADES-Au NCs might be due to the charge transfer effect caused by the chelation between Hg2+ and NADES surface oxygen-containing functional groups. Firstly, the surface of NADES-Au NCs modified with NADES was rich in oxygen-containing functional groups and hydrogen bonds system. The situation caused changes in surface states and the electron cloud density, resulting in reduced energy traps [41,42]. The average fluorescence lifetimes (Fig. 5B) of NADES-Au NCs decreased from 6.72 µs to 6.16 µs after the addition of Hg2+, indicating a dynamic quenching effect between the NADES-Au NCs and Hg2+. Afterwards, compared with the UV–vis spectrum of NADES-Au NCs, the blue shift and the enhancement of absorption peak at 275 nm of NADES-Au NCs + Hg2+ were attributed to the interaction between NADES-Au NCs and Hg2+ [43]. In addition, Hg2+ had no UV absorption and hardly overlapped with the fluorescence spectra of NADES-Au NCs, which ruled out the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The zeta potential was used to further confirm the presence of charge transfer. As shown in Fig. 5D, the zeta potential of NADES-Au NCs solution shifted from − 18.8 mV to − 6.4 mV with the addition of Hg2+, which consistently agreed with the results of UV–vis spectroscopy. So, the fluorescence quenching by Hg2+ was related to the charge transfer process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analytical performance of the method
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized parameters described above, the presented sensing system was investigated for detection of Hg2+ and 4-NA. The fluorescence responses of NADES-Au NCs in the presence of Hg2+ and 4-NA at different concentrations were recorded, and the linear calibration curves and the detection limits were measured. As pictured in Fig. 6 A, the emission intensity at 669 nm gradually decreased with the addition of various concentrations of Hg2+, while the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm basically unchanged. Simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity of NADES-Au NCs showed a good liner correlation with the concentration of Hg2+ in the range from 0.5 to 200 µM (F1/F2 = -0.01672 + 3.242[Hg2+], R2 = 0.9964) (Fig. 6B). And the lower detection limit was calculated as 0.25 µM (based on the 3σ/s). Furthermore, Fig. 6C depicted the variation trend of the fluorescence intensity of the NADES-Au NCs with the addition of 4-NA over a range concentration of 0–500 µM. In addition, Fig. 6D showed a clear linear plot in the range of 1–150 µM. The linear regression equation was y = -2.391 + 534.7[4-NA] (R2 = 0.9951), and the detection limit for 4-NA was 0.12 µM.

These results showed that the developed NADES-Au NCs probe had high sensitivity and wide dynamic range for both Hg2+ and 4-NA. More shockingly, the different responses of NADES-Au NCs at 440 and 669 nm changed from red to blue (Fig. 6E) and red to light (Fig. 6F) of the discriminative visual detection. Therefore, the two substances could be selectively identified by the different response signals at the double peaks, showing excellent sensing performance. The excellent characteristics of the NADES-Au NCs also indicated that it was comparable or better than those of most previous Hg2+-based or 4-NA-based detection methods (Table S1 and S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of the method
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity research was one of the important analytical indicators to estimate the performance of sensors. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the selective recognition capability of NADES-Au NCs for the determination of Hg2+ was demonstrated by comparing it with its analog (Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+). The fluorescence intensity of NADES-Au NCs at 440 nm and 669 nm remained unchanged with the addition of potential interfering substances, whereas addition of the Hg2+ dramatically quenched the fluorescence intensity of NADES-Au NCs at 669 nm. Furthermore, we evaluated the selectivity of fluorescent probe by studying the effects of some potential interfering substances, such as aniline (An), p-phenylenediamine (PPD), o-phenylenediamine (OPD) on the determination of nitroanilines (4-NA, 2, 4-NA and 2, 6-NA). The quenching effect of 4-NA and its analogues on the sensor were similar, but the quenching effect of 4-NA was significantly better (Fig. 7B). The red bar showed no significant interference with other coexisting compounds, which strongly demonstrated the superb selectivity of the proposed fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg2+ and nitroanilines.

The applicability of NADES-Au NCs was evaluated by detecting Hg2+ and 4-NA in real samples (tap and river water), where the contents of analytes were verified by a standard addition method. Certain concentrations of Hg2+ and 4-NA were directly spiked into the water samples, during which insoluble impurities were removed by a 0.2 µm membrane filtration. As shown in Table S3 and S4, it could be seen that the spiked recoveries of Hg2+ and 4-NA were satisfied over the range from 96.1 % to 112.2 % with RSD of 1.4–5.6 %. It showed that this method had good application potential in environmental monitoring.

In summary, for the first time, we have designed a novel NADES-Au NCs using NADES based on ChCl and Gly synthesis as solvents and stabilizers, with no additional steps required. Meanwhile, the as-prepared spongy NADES-Au NCs exhibited excellent fluorescence intensity and properties (pH stability and photobleaching), and could be used as a fast and green fluorescent sensor for the detection of Hg2+ and nitroanilines. Based on the charge transfer mechanism and the IFE, NADES-Au NCs was applied to detect Hg2+ and nitroanilines, and showed good linearities at 0.5–200 µM and 1–150 µM, respectively, with the low detection limits (0.2 µM and 0.12 µM). More interestingly, in addition to fast response, high sensitivity and selectivity, the sensor could distinguish Hg2+ from nitroanilines by the unique quenching type of fluorescence peak and was successfully applied to monitor the pollutants in tap water, lake water. We expect that this strategy may offer a novel synthesis method for Au NCs based on perspective of green chemistry, and construct other fluorescence detection platforms.

Jianqiang Song: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ning Zhao: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Longshan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011), Scientific Research Fund of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (2020LZD02), Inter-school Cooperation Project of General Undergraduate Universities in Liaoning Province (2020-181), Project of Shenyang Key Laboratory of Functional Drug Carrier Materials (Grant No. 19-110-4-08).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2023.121344.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.F.O. Maia, R.C. Hott, P.C.C. Ladeira, B.L. Batista, T.G. Andrade, M.S. Santos, M.C.S. Faria, L.C.A. Oliveira, D.S. Monteiro, M.C. Pereira, J.L. Rodrigues, Chemosphere 215 (2019) 422.
Y.Y. Li, J.T. Zhao, H. Zhong, Y. Wang, H. Li, Y. F. Li, V. Liem-Nguyen, T. Jiang, Z. Zhang, Y. Gao, Z. Chai, Environ. Sci. Technol 53 (2019) 1844-1852.
Y. Gao, T. Ma, Z. Ou, W. Cai, G. Yang, Y. Li, M. Xu, Q. Li, Talanta 178 (2018) 663.
